about.title: Galaxies
about.subtitle: 55,877 galaxies. Measured distances. One celestial sphere.

data.title: The Data
data.intro: This visualization is built on Cosmicflows-4, the largest compilation of galaxy distances ever assembled. Published by R. Brent Tully, Hélène Courtois, Ehsan Kourkchi and collaborators in 2023, it contains 55,877 galaxies with distances measured through direct observational methods — not estimates derived from the expansion of the universe.
data.methodsIntro: Each galaxy in the catalog has its distance determined by one or more of these methods, forming a cosmic distance ladder:

snia.name: Type Ia Supernovae
snia.desc: Standardizable explosions of white dwarf stars. Their consistent peak luminosity makes them visible across billions of light-years.

tf.name: Tully-Fisher
tf.desc: The faster a spiral galaxy rotates, the more luminous it is. Comparing intrinsic brightness to observed brightness gives the distance.

fp.name: Fundamental Plane
fp.desc: Elliptical galaxies follow a tight relationship between size, surface brightness, and velocity dispersion.

sbf.name: Surface Brightness Fluctuations
sbf.desc: Nearby galaxies look grainy; distant ones look smooth. The amplitude of pixel-to-pixel variations reveals the distance.

snii.name: Type II Supernovae
snii.desc: Core-collapse supernovae from massive stars. Their expanding photospheres can be used as geometric distance indicators.

trgb.name: Tip of the Red Giant Branch
trgb.desc: Red giant stars reach a maximum luminosity before helium ignition. This sharp cutoff in brightness is a reliable standard candle.

ceph.name: Cepheid Variables
ceph.desc: Pulsating stars whose period of brightening and dimming is directly related to their intrinsic luminosity. The original distance ladder rung.

mas.name: Masers
mas.desc: Water masers in galaxy accretion disks emit microwave radiation. VLBI observations give geometric distances with extraordinary precision.

mapping.title: From Data to Sky
mapping.positioning: Each galaxy is placed on a celestial sphere using its right ascension and declination — the same coordinate system astronomers use to locate objects in the night sky. You are looking outward from Earth, surrounded by the observable universe.
mapping.visibility: Not all 55,877 galaxies are visible at once. As you zoom in, the view reveals progressively more distant galaxies — from the local neighborhood (~560 galaxies) at the widest angle, down to the full deep field at maximum zoom. This redshift-based culling mirrors how real telescopes work: deeper exposures reveal fainter, more distant objects.
mapping.sizing: Galaxy size on screen is determined by measured distance. Closer galaxies appear larger, distant ones smaller — a direct representation of their physical proximity, not an arbitrary aesthetic choice.

rendering.title: Procedural Galaxy Rendering
rendering.intro: Cosmicflows-4 provides superb distance data but no morphological information — no galaxy type, no brightness magnitude, no diameter. Every galaxy you see in the detail view is procedurally generated.
rendering.morphology: Each galaxy is assigned a morphological type based on its PGC catalog number, using a seeded random function weighted by observed cosmic proportions: ~35% spiral, ~35% barred spiral, ~15% elliptical, ~10% lenticular, and ~5% irregular. The same PGC number always produces the same galaxy — the generation is deterministic.
rendering.particles: The detail view renders each galaxy as a particle system of tens of thousands of stars, shaped by morphology-specific parameters: spiral arm count and tightness, bar length, ellipticity, bulge size, and disk thickness. All parameters are derived from the seeded random generator.
rendering.blackhole: At the center of each galaxy, a supermassive black hole is rendered with an accretion disk and gravitational lensing effect. The lensing warps the surrounding starfield in a post-processing pass, with strength proportional to the camera zoom level.

dm.label: Distance Modulus
dm.desc: Combined weighted DM from all methods

map.title: Cosmic Map
map.infoIntro: You are looking at the three-dimensional distribution of 55,877 galaxies from the Cosmicflows-4 catalog, mapped in supergalactic coordinates. Instead of looking outward from Earth, you are now seeing the universe from outside — the actual spatial arrangement of galaxies in cosmic space.
map.infoStructure: The large-scale structure of the universe is not random. Galaxies cluster along filaments and sheets, surrounding vast empty voids. This network — the cosmic web — is the largest known pattern in nature, shaped by gravity over billions of years.
map.infoCoords: The axes show supergalactic Cartesian coordinates (SGX, SGY, SGZ), measured in km/s of recession velocity. This velocity-space representation preserves the observed redshift structure: nearby galaxies are near the origin, distant ones are far out.
map.infoColors: Colors encode CMB velocity — the galaxy's recession speed after correcting for our motion relative to the cosmic microwave background. Blue galaxies are nearby (slow recession), red and orange are far away (fast recession). This mirrors the color scheme used in the Tully et al. (2023) paper.
map.infoGroups: In Groups mode, galaxies are clustered by gravitational association — each point represents a galaxy group or cluster. In Galaxies mode, each of the 55,877 individual catalog entries is plotted separately.
